Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by Ibrahim Chaudry (OSB100)**

**Online Safety Bill Committee Submission**

Dear Online Safety Bill Committee,

I am writing to submit evidence in my capacity as an individual. However, aspects of this legislation concern me profoundly as the founder of a blog aimed for children and teenagers. It has been viewed over 16.2 million times by over 1.6 million people. Although these concerns are in no way reflective of each individual, the Online Safety Bill would significantly affect our online community as one that involves young people and young adults. While these provisions are well-intentioned, it feels regrettable that there has been a general acceptance of the regulations for "content created for and by children".

The key three concerns are:

**1) Sections 17(7) & 189(1)
**
Section 17(7) places a legal duty on providers to ensure children aren't able to access content which they're not supposed to - an example of which, from my understanding, would include social media needing to ensure that those who sign up are within the appropriate age requirement (ie. ensuring users are 16+). The legislation fails to clarify an important definition of "children", which was the exact same mistake that the European Union made when implementing similar requirements.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by Ibrahim Chaudry (OSB100)**

**Online Safety Bill Committee Submission**

Dear Online Safety Bill Committee,

I am writing to submit evidence in my capacity as an individual. However, aspects of this legislation concern me profoundly as the founder of a blog aimed for children and teenagers. It has been viewed over 16.2 million times by over 1.6 million people. Although these concerns are in no way reflective of each individual, the Online Safety Bill would significantly affect our online community as one that involves young people and young adults. While these provisions are well-intentioned, it feels regrettable that there has been a general acceptance of the regulations for "content created for and by children".

The key three concerns are:

**1) Sections 17(7) & 189(1)
**
Section 17(7) places a legal duty on providers to ensure children aren't able to access content which they're not supposed to - an example of which, from my understanding, would include social media needing to ensure that those who sign up are within the appropriate age requirement (ie. ensuring users are 16+). The legislation fails to clarify an important definition of "children", which was the exact same mistake that the European Union made when implementing similar requirements.

chunks.text: my understanding, would include social media needing to ensure that those who sign up are within the appropriate age requirement (ie. ensuring users are 16+). The legislation fails to clarify an important definition of "children", which was the exact same mistake that the European Union made when implementing similar requirements. Are individuals over the age of 18 who signed up to such services when they were a child still bound by such regulations? For example, would Twitter be required to remove the tweets of someone who is now an adult, yet published them when they were a child? Twitter infamously suspended tens of thousands of accounts when EU regulations came in, as they treated content based on the age of the user at the time it was published. It would be a tragedy if the Online Safety Bill were to require services to go even further in the United Kingdom. Fundamentally, the Bill should ensure that adults cannot have their content removed by a service on the basis of age. It fails to do so, and Section 189 should take the opportunity to clarify this. **2) Section 18 ** Given that young adults such as myself will likely be disproportionately affected by this Bill based on the above, some of the provisions relating to the appeals process seem concerning. It is comforting to see s18(3) explicitly confirm that all individuals, irrespective of age, will be entitled to appeal against a service if their content is removed. However, the process falls short in two key ways:
chunks.display_text: Are individuals over the age of 18 who signed up to such services when they were a child still bound by such regulations? For example, would Twitter be required to remove the tweets of someone who is now an adult, yet published them when they were a child? Twitter infamously suspended tens of thousands of accounts when EU regulations came in, as they treated content based on the age of the user at the time it was published. It would be a tragedy if the Online Safety Bill were to require services to go even further in the United Kingdom. Fundamentally, the Bill should ensure that adults cannot have their content removed by a service on the basis of age. It fails to do so, and Section 189 should take the opportunity to clarify this. **2) Section 18 ** Given that young adults such as myself will likely be disproportionately affected by this Bill based on the above, some of the provisions relating to the appeals process seem concerning. It is comforting to see s18(3) explicitly confirm that all individuals, irrespective of age, will be entitled to appeal against a service if their content is removed. However, the process falls short in two key ways:

chunks.text: on the above, some of the provisions relating to the appeals process seem concerning. It is comforting to see s18(3) explicitly confirm that all individuals, irrespective of age, will be entitled to appeal against a service if their content is removed. However, the process falls short in two key ways: \- There is no statutory time limit by which companies must address an appeal - the Data Protection Act 2018 already requires private companies to respond to Subject Access Requests within 30 days, yet this Bill falls short of even placing a timeframe \- There is no suggestion as to whether appeals need to be looked at by a human or if a service could automatically reject any appeal While it is the case that this provides more provisions to users than already exist, that argument is disingenuous: if the Online Safety Bill places a legal duty on services to remove more content, then it is inevitable services will remove more harmless and lawful content. It is important that the appeals process contains the mechanisms to empower individual users if this affects them. **3) Section 2(1) ** It is disappointing to see the Online Safety Bill not take into account how communication is shared in a "user-to-user service". In effect, the current proposals would require blogs such as my own to be considered such a service, even though every piece of content written by users is manually monitored and approved. It would be far more reasonable for the Bill's definition to only consider a service one to be "user-to-user" if the communication is transmitted in real-time.
chunks.display_text: \- There is no statutory time limit by which companies must address an appeal - the Data Protection Act 2018 already requires private companies to respond to Subject Access Requests within 30 days, yet this Bill falls short of even placing a timeframe \- There is no suggestion as to whether appeals need to be looked at by a human or if a service could automatically reject any appeal While it is the case that this provides more provisions to users than already exist, that argument is disingenuous: if the Online Safety Bill places a legal duty on services to remove more content, then it is inevitable services will remove more harmless and lawful content. It is important that the appeals process contains the mechanisms to empower individual users if this affects them. **3) Section 2(1) ** It is disappointing to see the Online Safety Bill not take into account how communication is shared in a "user-to-user service". In effect, the current proposals would require blogs such as my own to be considered such a service, even though every piece of content written by users is manually monitored and approved. It would be far more reasonable for the Bill's definition to only consider a service one to be "user-to-user" if the communication is transmitted in real-time.

chunks.text: such as my own to be considered such a service, even though every piece of content written by users is manually monitored and approved. It would be far more reasonable for the Bill's definition to only consider a service one to be "user-to-user" if the communication is transmitted in real-time. I hope that my evidence will be accepted, and I would be happy to provide additional written or oral clarification upon request - even at short notice - if it would be beneficial to the Committee. I am very passionate about ensuring this legislation fulfils its aims, yet it still remains seriously flawed. Thank you very much for your time. Best wishes, \-Ibrahim Chaudry *June 2022*

I am very passionate about ensuring this legislation fulfils its aims, yet it still remains seriously flawed. Thank you very much for your time. Best wishes, \-Ibrahim Chaudry *June 2022*
